coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots assembled SBA-15 and its oxygen sensing properties
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Bingfu
Wang, Li
Zhang, Haoran
Liu, Yingliang
Dong, Hanwu
Zheng, Mingtao
Zhou, Xiaohua
coredata.dc:description: A simple, environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach has been developed to assemble luminescence carbon dots (CDs) into the ordered channels of the mesoporous SBA-15. When analyzing the effect of gases such as nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide and water vapor in the air on the luminescent properties of CDs/SBA-15 hybrid material, we found that the as-prepared samples had a reversible oxygen sensing signal and good photochemical stability. The samples had no response to carbon dioxide, or nitrogen, indicating that the material has extremely high selectivity to the oxygen over the other gases, and water vapor had almost no effect on the luminescent properties of the CDs/SBA-15 material. The response time was determined to be about 7–10s and the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increased. These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in wide concentration range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent carbon dots
Ordered mesopore structure
Oxygen sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The oxygen sensing test schematic of CDs/SBA-15.
The small angle XRD patterns of as-synthesized SBA-15 (a), CDs/SBA-15 (0.5%, b), CDs/SBA-15(3%, c), CDs/SBA-15 (7%, d), CDs/SBA-15 (9%, e).
FTIR spectra for as-synthesized SBA-15 (a), surfactant-extracted SBA-15 (b), CDs/SBA-15 (1%,c), and CDs/SBA-15(5%, d).
FESEM images of as-synthesized SBA-15 (a), FESEM and TEM images of surfactant-extracted SBA-15 (b, d), CDs/SBA-15(1%) (c, e).

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and DFT pore size distribution curve of surfactant-extracted SBA-15 (a), CDs/SBA-15 (1%) (b), CDs/SBA-15 (3%) (c), CDs/SBA-15 (5%) (d), CDs/SBA-15 (7%) (e) and CDs/SBA-15 (9%) (f).

Excitation and emission spectra of CDs/SBA-15 (λ ex =455nm), (The picture was shot in total darkness for carbon dot, the picture b was taken under ultraviolet light for carbon dot, the picture c was taken under ultraviolet light for different concentrations of CDs assembly SBA-15 samples).

Typical luminescence intensity-based Stern–Volmer plots for different concentrations of CDs assembled SBA-15 samples (The left side of this figure). The solid lines presents the best fit using the two-site model Eq. (2). Emission spectra of the CDs/SBA-15 (7%) sample measured at different oxygen volume fractions (λ ex =455nm) (The right side of this figure).

Response time and relative intensity change and reproducibility for SBA-15-xCDs on switching between 100% nitrogen (k1) and 100% oxygen (k2), (a, x =0.5%; b, x =1%; c, x =3%; d, x =5%; e, x =7%; f, x =9%).
Response time and relative intensity change and reproducibility for SBA-15-CDs (5%) on switching between 100% carbon dioxide (P1) and 100% nitrogen (P2).
Textural data of different concentrations of CDs assembly SBA-15.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a 0: cell parameter, a 0 =2d100/√3; S BET: BET specific surface area; V: pore volume; D: pore diameter; t: wall thickness, t = a 0 −D.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Values of response (I 0/I 100), t↓, t↑, Stern–Volmer model and Demas model oxygen-quenching fitting parameters of CDs/SBA-15.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots assembled SBA-15 and its oxygen sensing properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach has been developed to assemble luminescence carbon dots (CDs) into the ordered channels of the mesoporous SBA-15. When analyzing the effect of gases such as nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide and water vapor in the air on the luminescent properties of CDs/SBA-15 hybrid material, we found that the as-prepared samples had a reversible oxygen sensing signal and good photochemical stability. The samples had no response to carbon dioxide, or nitrogen, indicating that the material has extremely high selectivity to the oxygen over the other gases, and water vapor had almost no effect on the luminescent properties of the CDs/SBA-15 material. The response time was determined to be about 7–10s and the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increased. These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in wide concentration range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, various materials have been proposed for oxygen sensing or detection. The ruthenium (II) complex covalently assembled mesoporous MSU-3 silica [1] showed fast response to oxygen, but these ruthenium (II) complexes are very expensive and these synthesis processes are complicated and time-consumed. Compared with other inorganic nanomaterials, the photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) can be prepared by a simple, low cost and eco-friendly method [2]. An important advantage of CDs is that, carbon is generally not considered as a toxic element, unlike the semi-conductor QDs containing heavy metals. CDs have aroused intense interests by researchers and soon become a star material among a diversity of nanomaterials since its serendipitous discovery [3–5]. When inorganic carbon sources (mainly activated carbon) are adopted as raw materials, the obtained CDs possess low quantum yield and poor reproducibility. CDs with high quantum yield are often prepared through organic carbon sources [6] or typically citric acid. Fluorescent CDs exhibit great potential in biosensing and bioimaging due to their nontoxicity, biocompatibility, excellent chemical- and photo-stability as well as superior optical properties [7]. Among the possible matrix materials for loading of CDs, silica gel and organically modified silicates are particularly attractive due to their inherent stability and optical properties, which would allow direct incorporation into existing lighting formats. Normally, to avoid phase separation, aggregation or low doping concentration, the preparation of nanomaterials or polymer hybrid materials can be modified with organosilanes after their formation [8].

In an oxygen sensing material, a high diffusion coefficient of gas is necessary for a rapid response. A highly and locally effective quenching around the oxygen probe is useful for good response [9,10]. The discovery of M41S-type ordered mesoporous materials opened a new class of periodically porous solids. SBA-15 is a material containing mesopores which are internally connected by micropores across the hexagonally arranged silica walls [11]. And SBA-15 is more stable than MCM-41 due to the thicker silica walls and ligands can be immobilized into the ordered mesopores [12]. The studies on mesoporous silica have been one of the most vibrant fields in materials science due to the thicker wall, uniform pore size, large surface area and high chemical stability [13], which are excellent for sensor, catalysis, drug delivery and bio-medical applications [14–20].

SBA-15 is well suited as a structure that can contain individual photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles and metal particles with mesopores, and the pores are wide enough to permit facile diffusion of reactants and products. There is no literature showing that loading of CDs into the channels of mesoporous materials SBA-15. The photoluminescent CDs assembled SBA-15 mesoporous materials had a potential for gas sensing application, because its highly ordered channel structure can promote the gas diffusion effectively.

Herein, we successfully prepared SBA-15 by hydrothermal method. Direct impregnation method was used to incorporate the CDs into SBA-15, and its oxygen sensing properties was investigated. All results indicate that this system can be used for oxygen detection in the whole range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentration. The samples show an excellent oxygen selectivity and oxygen quenching response. All these exceptional properties make the CDs/SBA-15 system qualified for sensitive and jam-proof oxygen sensors. More importantly, the approach described here can be easily adopted to prepare other organometallic quantum dots assembled devices with high performances. We hope our work can inspire the development of new type of oxygen sensing materials and devices.

Triblock copolymer poly(ethylene glycol)-block-poly(propylene glycol)-block-poly-(ethylene glycol) (Pluronic P123; EO20PO70EO20; m.w.=5800) was purchased from Aldrich. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was supplied from Tianjin Chemicals Co. Ltd. Ethanol (99.7%, Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co. Ltd.) was used without any purification. N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-ammonia propyl methyl dimethoxy silane (N-aminoethyl-3-ammonia propyl methyl dimethoxy silane, Silane Coupler KH-602, m.w.=206.358) was from Guangzhou Longkai Co. Ltd. Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%, AR) was purchased from Aladdin. De-ionized water was used throughout this work.

6.0g P123 was completely dissolved in 180mL of 2M aqueous HCl and 45mL of deionized water by stirring at 308K. And then 13.8mL tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, Tianjin Chemicals Co.) was added dropwise to the solution and stirred for 24h at 298K. Next, the reaction stands for 48h in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 373K. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was filtered by Buchner funnel, thoroughly washed with distilled water five times and ethanol one time, and dried at 333K in an oven. The powder was transferred to a corundum crucible and calcined at 823K in furnace for 48h to remove the surfactant.

According to Ref. [21], 30mL Silane Coupler KH-602 were placed into a 100mL three-necked flask and degassed with nitrogen for 10min and was heated to 513K. 1.5g anhydrous citric acid was quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring for 3min. The final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times.

Scheme 1 shows the preparation process of CDs/SBA-15. 0.4g surfactant removed SBA-15 was immersed in 30mL chloroform, CDs was added to the solution with stirring for 8h. The precipitate was filtered by Buchner funnel, and dried at 333K in an oven. The samples were denoted as SBA-15-xCDs (x =0.5%, 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 8%, 9%, 10%, 15%), where x is wt% concentration of CDs.

Crystal structure of samples was analyzed by small angle powder X-ray diffraction (SAXRD, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418Å). The data was collected in a 2θ range from 0.6° to 4° with a scanning step of 0.02° and a scanning rate of 0.2°/min. Infrared spectrum was recorded on Fourier spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere, using KBr as reference. The particle morphology was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL-30; Philips, North Billerica, MA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, TECNAI12, Holland). Nitrogen physisorption isotherms were measured using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020HD88 system. The pore size distributions were calculated from the DFT pore size distribution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2nm) and a 150W Xe lamp as the excitation source, with the emission at 370nm as the excitation wavelength.

The oxygen-sensing properties based on luminescence intensity quenching of the CDs/SBA-15 were characterized using the previously reported method [22]. Scheme 2 shows the configuration of oxygen sensing measurement of CDs/SBA-15. Before the measurement, 0.2g powder samples were first pressed by a slide into pellets with 10mm in diameter and 1mm in thickness. For the Stern–Volmer plots measurements, oxygen and nitrogen were mixed at different concentrations via gas-flow controllers and flew directly into the gas chamber sealed with a closely fitting suba-seal rubber lid equipped with two (IN and OUT) tubes, and the results were recorded by the same Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

A series of small angle XRD patterns for pristine calcined SBA-15 and different concentrations of CDs assembled SBA-15 samples are shown in Fig. 1 . In the low angle region, peaks can be indexed as (100), (110) and (210), which confirms a typical hexagonally ordered mesoporous structure of SBA-15 [23]. The ordered structure was retained after the CDs had been loaded, and with the increase of CDs concentration, the intensity of (100), (110) and (210) reduced [24] and the latter two gradually disappeared completely, suggesting that the mesopores are largely filled [25]. The reduction of the diffraction intensities decreased with the increase of the CDs concentration is considered to be contributed from two factors: (1) lower order of the material at mesoscale (such as the change of the thickness of the pore wall); (2) the interaction between the amorphous silica network in the hole wall and the organic parts which leads to the variation of the mesopores structure [26].

Mesoporous materials SBA-15 at various preparation stages were investigated by Fourier transformation infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy to search for the mechanism of loading of CDs into SBA-15. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2 display the typical SiOSi bands of the inorganic framework. The SiOSi bands of all spectra can be proved from the vibration peak around 1088cm−1 (ν as, SiO), 802cm−1 (ν s, SiO), 454cm−1, 456cm−1, 460cm−1 (δ, SiOSi). The wide vibration peak around 3440cm−1 belongs to ν (SiOH) and ν (OH) in absorbed water, and 1634cm−1 for δ (OH) in water. In the spectrum (b) the absorption peaks of ν (CH) bond (2700–3000cm−1) [27] and the peaks of δ as (CH) bond (1380–1460cm−1) become weak indicating the template agent has been removed. In the spectra (c and d), with the increase of the CDs concentration, the vibration peaks (1515–1680cm−1) derived from amide group (-CONR-) in CDs increase obviously, which confirms the CDs were doped into the mesoporous materials SBA-15. (ν =stretching vibration, δ =bending vibration, s =symmetric vibration, as=antisymmetric vibration).

Morphologies of SBA-15 and CDs/SBA-15 were characterized by FESEM and TEM. The representative FESEM and TEM images reveal that the P6mm hexagonal mesoporous structure with long rang orders of SBA-15 (Fig. 3 b and d) and the structure is preserved after the CDs were loaded (Fig. 3c and e). As can be seen from Fig. a, there are plenty of micropores on the P6mm hexagonal structure which are thought to be originated from insertion of the PEO chains. The SBA-15 was prepared using the triblock copolymer P123, which is amphiphilic molecular consists of two hydrophilic PEO chains and a hydrophobic PPO chain, as template. A preferential polycondensation and deposition of inorganic species around hydrophilic PEO chains gradually developed the ordered SBA-15 walls. At the same time, the hydrophilic PEO chains, mostly distributed in the water, can be inserted into the hole wall of SiO2. Fig. 3b shows that the surfactant P123 was fully removed through calcination at 823K in furnace for 48h. Results from TEM are in consistent with the small angle XRD, which further prove the hexagonal mesoporous structure of CDs/SBA-15.

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were used to access surface area, pore volume, pore diameter, etc. of these samples. The isotherms of all materials exhibit type IV behavior with type H1 hysteresis loop signifying mesostructured arrangement of SBA-15 in Fig. 4
[28]. The mesopores were generated by calcinations which removes the P123 template. Size of the micelles ranges from 10 to 15nm (Fig. 4a), and the diameter of the mesopores loaded with luminescence CDs ranges from 6 to 12nm (Fig. 4b–f). The pore size becomes increasingly narrow after doping CDs, which is attributed to the confinement effect of CDs inside the channels of SBA-15. The specific surface area and pore volume gradually decreased as the concentration of CDs increases and then modest increased at higher concentrations in Table 1 , because the channels were fully filled with CDs.

Fig. 5 presents the excitation and emission spectra of CDs/SBA-15 with 370nm as the excitation wavelength and 455nm as the emission wavelength at room temperature. The wavelength range of excitation light was from 230nm to 440nm in the experiment. The maximum emission peak intensity value was found in the sample of 7%. We believe that the maximum 7% CDs loading concentration is due to the optimized CDs grafting onto the backbone of the silica matrix. Below 7%, more CDs loading on the surface of the silica will obviously increase the luminescence intensity, above this point, the silica backbone of the SBA-15 will contain increasing CDs portion, which inevitably cause the decreasing in the mesoscopic order of the hexagonal mesostructure. This point can be proven by the XRD pattern, as shown Fig. 1. Due to the light scatting of the SBA-15 silica backbone and the concentration quenching of CDs cause by aggregation, the CDs highly loaded CDs/SBA-15 samples (above 7%) only present slightly increasing emission intensity.

Fig. 6 (the right side of the figure) shows the emission spectra of sample CDs/SBA-15 (7%) measured at different oxygen concentrations at room temperature. There are no obvious changes in the spectrum shape and peak position at different oxygen volume fraction for all samples. However, the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increases. The degree of quenching for the SBA-15-CDs (0.5%, 1%, 3%, 5%, 7% and 9%) samples reach 43.06%, 74.16%, 72.89%, 75.41%, 61.26% and 65.08%, respectively. The emission peaks intensity values became higher as doping CDs concentration increased for a certain level of concentrations of oxygen. These results showed that the CDs/SBA-15 had a great potential for oxygen sensing application.

Luminescent molecule quenching in homogeneous media with negligible matrix effects are expected to show the dependence of emission intensity or decay time on oxygen concentration, which can be described by the Stern–Volmer (SV) equation:
(1)
I
0
I
=
τ
0
τ
= 1 +
K sv
P
o 2
= 1 + k
τ
0
P
O 2
where I and τ are the luminescence intensity and the excited-state lifetime of the related decay profile, respectively. The subscript 0 denotes the absence of oxygen. K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant. k is the bimolecular rate constant describing the efficiency of the collisional encounters between the luminophore and the quencher, and P O2 is the O2 concentration. In the ideal case, the plot of I 0/I or τ 0/τ versus oxygen concentration should give a straight-line relationship with slope K SV.

As you can see from Fig. 6 (the left side of the figure), the quenching was found to be appreciable and a non-linear curve with downward curvature was obtained in the SV plot. The light-emitting molecule is unevenly distributed in the pore channels of mesoporous SBA-15, which are distributed over two different sites. Luminescence molecules in different sites with various K SV and τ 0 values can be attributed to the distribution of sites for micro-heterogeneous. Luminescence molecules in different sites, characterized by different degrees of oxygen quenching, which result in nolinear Stern–Volmer response curve [29–32]. In this case, the linear response Eq. (1) is not enough to fit the Stern–Volmer plots because of their downward curvature. Several models have been proposed to explain such nonlinearity, and the downward oxygen sensing Stern–Volmer plots can be well fitted by the Demas two-site model. The straight-line intensity Stern–Volmer Eq. (1) can be derived as two-site model equation [30]:
(2)
I
0
I
=
1
f
01
/ 1 +
K
S V 1
p
02
+
f
02
/ 1 +
K
S V 2
p
02
where f 01 and f 02 denote the fractional contributions of the total intensity from the luminescence molecules located at two different sites that exhibit two discrete Stern–Volmer constants K SV1 and K SV2, respectively.

The higher values of Ksv and the lower values of f 01 for SBA-15 doped with CDs (1%, 3%, 5%) show a higher oxygen quenching response in Table 2 . However, combined with all the mentioned results (including the mesoscopic order of the mesostructure, the response and the long-term stability) within this manuscript, we think that the 5% CDs loaded SBA-15 sample is the best. The recovery time, response time and stability are also very important factors in evaluating oxygen sensor properties. Traditionally, 95% response time, i.e., t↓ (95%, N2 →O2) refers to the time required for the luminescent intensity to decrease by 95% on changing from 100% nitrogen to 100% oxygen, and 95% recovery time, i.e., t↑ (95% O2 →N2), is defined as the time required for the luminescent intensity to reach 95% of the initial value recorded under 100% nitrogen on changing from 100% oxygen to 100% nitrogen. Fig. 7 shows the emission intensity of the samples drops quickly with the increasing of oxygen concentration, and recovers when 100% nitrogen replacing oxygen. The values of response (I 0/I 100), t↓ and t↑ parameters are compiled in Table 2. Fig. 8 shows the small differences in fluorescence intensity of the sample SBA-15-CDs (5%) among nitrogen and carbon dioxide gas atmosphere. From Fig. 7, the baseline signal of the mesoporous materials SBA-15 doped with CDs was quite stable and reversible, there was also a rapid response. This feature can be attributed to highly ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier which can promote the gas diffusion effectively. In addition, in order to detect the response of water vapor to samples, we dried gases (nitrogen, carbon dioxide and oxygen) with a desiccant and ran the same test of the sensing properties which resulted in only slightly varied experimental data. It is confirmed by several parallel experiments that these samples prepared in the same way show similar results. The samples had no response to carbon dioxide, or nitrogen, thus indicating that the material had extremely high selectivity to oxygen over the other gases, and water vapor almost had no effect on the test results of the material CDs/SBA-15. During our experimental processes, their long-term stability was also closely monitored. The average response stability of the assembled mesostructured silica was measured over 4 months at every 15 days and these results revealed that the effect of aging time on the average O2 sensing response stability is small enough to be neglected. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the response (I 0/I) was 2.2%, which is below the accuracy (±4%) of the Hitachi-F7000 spectrophotometer. The excellent long-term stability and sustained high response arise from the increased flexibility that overcomes the pore shrinkage and/or collapse with time due to the covalent bond between the silica and the carbon dots.

Mesoporous hybrid luminescent material SBA-15 doped with CDs was prepared. The CDs assembled SBA-15 is a potential material in oxygen sensing fields due to its nontoxic, biocompatible, excellent chemical and photostability as well as superior optical properties. In the course of analyzing the prime gas such as nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide and water vapor in the air effecting on the hybrid material CDs/SBA-15, we found that the as-prepared samples had a reversible oxygen sensing signal and a good photochemical stability. The samples had no response to carbon dioxide, or nitrogen, thus indicating that the material had extremely high selectivity to the oxygen over the other gases, and water vapor almost had no effect on the test results of the material CDs/SBA-15. The response time was determined to be about 7–10s and the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increases. The emission intensity of the samples drops quickly with the increasing of oxygen concentration, and recovers when 100% nitrogen replacing oxygen. The resulting response signal of the samples was stable and reversible during the long term test (over 4 months). These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in the whole range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 21571067), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen-sensing materials based on ruthenium (II) complex covalently assembled mesoporous MSU-3 silica

Physically flexible, rapid-response gas sensor based on colloidal quantum dot solids
Triblock copolymer syntheses of mesoporous silica with periodic 50 to 300 angstrom pores

Rhodamine group modified SBA-15 fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of Hg2+ and its application as an INHIBIT logic device
Nanoporous activated carbon derived from Lapsi (Choerospondias axillaris) seed stone for the removal of arsenic from water
Extrusion of AlSBA-15 molecular sieves: an industrial point of view
Preparation of highly ordered mesoporous AlSBA-15 and its application to isopropylation of m-cresol
Benefits of electronic wiring and spacers on lithium storage in nanostructured lithium-ion battery anodes
Functionalized mesoporous silica materials for controlled drug delivery
Mesoporous silica nanoparticles in biomedical applications
Mesostructured silica chemically doped with RuII as a superior optical oxygen sensor
Synergistic adsorption of As(V) from aqueous solution onto mesoporous silica decorated orderly with Al2O3 and Fe2O3 nanoparticles
Modification of SBA-15 pore connectivity by high-temperature calcination investigated by carbon inverse replication
Acidity and catalytic behaviors of ordered mesoporous aluminosilicate materials containing zeolite building units
Incorporation of cyclodextrin into mesostructured silica
Ion-imprinted zeolite: a surface functionalization methodology based on the ship-in-bottle technique

Nanostructured oxygen sensor-using micelles to incorporate a hydrophobic platinum porphyrin

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bingfu Lei received his BSc in 2001 in Food and Science Engineering and MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from (Jinan University) JNU, and PhD in 2007 in Condensed Matter Physics from Changchun Institute of Optics Fine Mechanics and Physics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and then he became a JSPS Postdoctoral Fellowship for Foreign Researchers at the Center for Advanced Science and Innovation of Osaka University from 2007 to 2009. He obtained a professor position at South China Agricultural University (SCAU) since 2012. His current research interests mainly focuses on the exploration of covalently grafted hybrid materials as gas sensors, chemosensors and high luminescent display devices. He also interests in the research of spectral converting and electrical-optical converting luminescent materials that using in agricultural field aiming at improving the light quality for plant photosynthesis.

Li Wang received her BSc of Chemical Engineering and Technology from Wuyi University, Jiangmen, China in 2014, and then she began her Master of Applied Chemistry student experience under the supervising of Prof. Bingfu Lei since 2014 in SCAU. Her current research is the synthesis and characterization of nanoporous organic-inorganic hybrid materials for optical sensing

Haoran Zhang received her MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from Northeast Normal University in China, and then she received her PhD in 2011 in Jinan University (JNU). Then she has been working as a lectorship at SCAU. Her current research is synthesis and application of organic-inorganic hybrid compound used for sensing and display.

Yingliang Liu received his MSc degree in Chemistry in 1989 from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and PhD in 1994 in Inorganic Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University. He has been a professor at the JNU since 2002 and moved from JNU to SCAU at 2012. He has been working on the topics including luminescence materials, functional glass and photoelectron materials.

Hanwu Dong received his Bachelor’s degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Harbin Engineering University in 2004. In 2008, he was funded by China Scholarship Council and studied as a PhD student at Structural Chemistry, Arrhenius Laboratory, Stockholm University for two years. He obtained his PhD in Materials Processing and Engineering from South China University of Technology in 2011, and then he has been working as a lectorship at SCAU. His research concerns about preparation and structures of phosphor materials and energy materials and the applications of these materials, especially biomass-derived carbon materials for energy storage.

Mingtao Zheng received his BSc of Chemistry Education from Hainan Normal University in 2000, and MSc in 2007 in Inorganic Chemistry from JNU, and he has received his PhD of Biomedical Engineering in 2011 in JNU, and then he has been working as a lectorship at SCAU. His current research interests mainly focus on biomass materials.

Xiaohua Zhou received her BSc of Analytical Chemistry from Jilin University in 1992, and MSc in 1995 in Inorganic Chemistry from the same university. She has worked as research scholar and chemistry faculty in SCAU since 1995. Now she is an associate professor and her main research interests are concerned with nucleic fluorescent probes, nanostructured microspheres, as well as drug controlled release carrier.
